svg.text: 6. The exposed core survives as a neutron star — or, if it is heavy
enough, as a black hole.
What remains at the centre is a neutron star: a sphere of almost pure neu-
trons holding one to two times the mass of the Sun inside a ball little more
than twenty kilometres across.
A Spoonful Weighing a Billion
Tonnes
Density is the quality that sets a neutron star apart from anything in ordinary
experience. Compressing the mass of the Sun into the volume of a small city
pushes matter to roughly the density of an atomic nucleus — several hundred
trillion times denser than water. A single teaspoon of neutron-star material
would weigh about as much as a mountain range.
Yet a neutron star is far from a featureless blob. Gravity and nuclear forces
sort it into distinct layers. A wafer-thin atmosphere of hot plasma sits atop a
solid crust of ions locked in a crystalline lattice. Deeper down, nuclei grow
so neutron-rich that neutrons begin to drip out of them, and the matter con-
torts into exotic shapes nicknamed ‘nuclear pasta.’ Below the crust lies a vast
core of superfluid neutrons, and at the very centre — at densities beyond any-
thing physics has pinned down — matter may dissolve into free quarks or other
states no laboratory has produced.
